**Dalian Shide** was a professional Chinese [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Dalian](/wiki/Dalian "Dalian"), [Liaoning](/wiki/Liaoning "Liaoning") province, China who played in China's football league system between 1955 and 2012. Their home stadiums were the 55,843 capacity [Dalian People's Stadium](/wiki/Dalian_People%27s_Stadium "Dalian People's Stadium") and then later in 1997 they moved to the 30,776 capacity [Jinzhou Stadium](/wiki/Jinzhou_Stadium "Jinzhou Stadium").

The club was initially founded in 1955 as **Dalian Shipyards** and made sporadic appearances within the Chinese national leagues until 1982 when the local Dalian government took ownership of the club and renamed it **Dalian Football Club**. The club won their first major silverware when they won the 1992 domestic cup title. In 1993, the club was reorganised to become a completely professional football team, renamed themselves **Dalian Wanda FC** and went on to win the first fully professional [1994 Chinese Jia-A League](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_1994 "Chinese Jia-A League 1994") title.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The tycoon [Xu Ming](/wiki/Xu_Ming "Xu Ming") and the [Shide Group](/wiki/Shide_Group "Shide Group") would go on to take over the club rename it Dalian Shide.

Achieving a total of eight league titles from both the [Jia A](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League "Chinese Jia-A League") and the rebranded [CSL](/wiki/Chinese_Super_League "Chinese Super League") Dalian were the most successful club in Chinese football, while in the [Asian Football Confederation](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation") the club reached the [1997–98 Asian Club Championship](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Asian_Club_Championship "1997–98 Asian Club Championship") and [2000–01 Asian Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Asian_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "2000–01 Asian Cup Winners' Cup") finals.

The club accumulated a huge debt and was eventually sold and merged into another club, thus becoming defunct.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dalian_Shide_F.C.&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The club was founded as **Dalian Shipyards** in 1955. After the 1982 season the local Dalian government took over its ownership and renamed it **Dalian Football Club** to represent the city. The club immediately became a major force within the second tier and eventually win promotion to the top level in 1984 when they won the division title.[[3]](#cite_note-3) For the next several seasons Dalian were predominantly top tier regulars except for one season in 1989 when they were in the second tier, however they quickly won promotion and soon went on to win their first domestic cup in 1992.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/e1/Dalian_Shide_F.C.1994-1999.svg/250px-Dalian_Shide_F.C.1994-1999.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Dalian_Shide_F.C.1994-1999.svg)

Dalian Wanda logo used between 1994 and 1999

Soon afterwards the [Chinese Football Association](/wiki/Chinese_Football_Association "Chinese Football Association") started to demand full professionalism and sponsorship from all the clubs in China. Dalian went on to be one of the first fully professionalised clubs in China on July 3, 1992. They gained sponsorship in 1993 from the [China Hualu group](/wiki/China_Hualu_group "China Hualu group") and then the [Dalian Wanda Group](/wiki/Dalian_Wanda_Group "Dalian Wanda Group") on March 8, 1994, who changed the club's name to **Dalian Wanda FC**.
In the inaugural fully professional [1994 league season](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_1994 "Chinese Jia-A League 1994"), the club brought in former [Chinese national team](/wiki/China_PR_national_football_team "China PR national football team") manager [Zhang Honggen](/wiki/Zhang_Honggen "Zhang Honggen") to coach the team and under his leadership guide the club to their first ever league title.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The following season saw Zhang Honggen decide to not stay on as the club's manager and Dalian were unable to retain the league title, however the club eventually brought in former Chinese international player [Chi Shangbin](/wiki/Chi_Shangbin "Chi Shangbin") into the club as their new manager. With [Wang Jianlin](/wiki/Wang_Jianlin "Wang Jianlin") and the full support of his company the Dalian Wanda Group taking full ownership of the club, they started to build a football dynasty by winning the [1996 league season](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_1996 "Chinese Jia-A League 1996") undefeated.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Domestic dominance continued in the [1997 league season](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_1997 "Chinese Jia-A League 1997") while Dalian narrowly just missed out on [1997–98 Asian Club Championship](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Asian_Club_Championship "1997–98 Asian Club Championship") and Chi Shangbin decided to leave to take on a position with the Chinese national team. Despite this Dalian had a well-funded and prolific academy that produced numerous Chinese internationals such as [Zhang Enhua](/wiki/Zhang_Enhua "Zhang Enhua"), [Li Ming](/wiki/Li_Ming_(footballer,_born_1971) "Li Ming (footballer, born 1971)") and [Sun Jihai](/wiki/Sun_Jihai "Sun Jihai") and with another former Chinese national team manager [Xu Genbao](/wiki/Xu_Genbao "Xu Genbao") Dalian were easily able to replicate their success by winning the [1998 league title](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_1998 "Chinese Jia-A League 1998").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 2000 at the height of their success, Wang Jianlin decided to pull out from football after publicly criticizing Chinese referees for match fixing and he did not return to football until 2011 when his associated Wanda Group sponsored the [2011 Chinese Super League](/wiki/2011_Chinese_Super_League "2011 Chinese Super League") after former Chinese FA Vice-chairmen Xie Yalong and Nan Yong stood on trial for bribery.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

On January 9, 2000, Wang Jianlin sold Dalian to [Xu Ming](/wiki/Xu_Ming "Xu Ming") and the Shide Group for 120 million [Yuan](/wiki/Renminbi "Renminbi") and the club's name was changed to **Dalian Shide**. The new owners brought in [Serbian](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") [Milorad Kosanović](/wiki/Milorad_Kosanovi%C4%87 "Milorad Kosanović") as their new manager and win another league title in the [2000 league season](/wiki/Chinese_Jia-A_League_2000 "Chinese Jia-A League 2000"). Kosanović made the men's team by far the most successful team in Chinese football by winning the 2001, 2002 league title, 2001 [Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/Chinese_FA_Cup "Chinese FA Cup") and just missing out on [2000–01 Asian Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Asian_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "2000–01 Asian Cup Winners' Cup") during his reign.[[9]](#cite_note-9) When Milorad Kosanović left to coach the [Serbia and Montenegro national under-21 football team](/wiki/Serbia_and_Montenegro_national_under-21_football_team "Serbia and Montenegro national under-21 football team") the club eventually brought in [Vladimir Petrović](/wiki/Vladimir_Petrovi%C4%87 "Vladimir Petrović") who guided the team to the recently rebranded [2005 Chinese Super League](/wiki/2005_Chinese_Super_League "2005 Chinese Super League") title as well as 2005 Chinese FA Cup. With the retirement of striker [Hao Haidong](/wiki/Hao_Haidong "Hao Haidong") and Vladimir Petrović leaving to take the Chinese national team management position, the club went through an inconsistent period due to team and coach changes. In 2008, Dalian Shide selected a number of players from its academy to play in a satellite team in the S.League in Singapore, called [Dalian Shide Siwu FC](/wiki/Dalian_Shide_Siwu_FC "Dalian Shide Siwu FC").

On 14 February 2010, [Zhang Yalin](/wiki/Zhang_Yalin "Zhang Yalin") died of [lymphoma](/wiki/Lymphoma "Lymphoma") in [Dalian](/wiki/Dalian "Dalian"), [Liaoning](/wiki/Liaoning "Liaoning"), aged 28, after a two-year-battle with the disease.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On 30 November 2012, Dalian Shide were acquired by Aerbin Group and merged into [Dalian Aerbin F.C.](/wiki/Dalian_Aerbin_F.C. "Dalian Aerbin F.C."),[[11]](#cite_note-11) although a lot of confusion and rumour surrounded the demise of Shide.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Grounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dalian_Shide_F.C.&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Grounds")]

Dalian played in the 55,843-seat multi-purpose [Dalian People's Stadium](/wiki/Dalian_People%27s_Stadium "Dalian People's Stadium") for much of their early history.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 1997 the club moved to the recently built 31,000 seat multi-use [Jinzhou Stadium](/wiki/Jinzhou_Stadium "Jinzhou Stadium") in [Dalian](/wiki/Dalian "Dalian"), [China](/wiki/China "China").[[14]](#cite_note-14)
